author: luca_zanolini
content: Not sure if given the attack (and assuming a builder has that power, which seems unlikely), this would solve it tho? The idea of the attack is that the builder splits the network such that the validators that get the right payload will get it 'too late' so that the proposer does not have time to retrieve it before proposing, and it will build on EMPTY. Would this change solve this?

author: luca_zanolini
content: I'm writing the proofs given the assumption I wrote above (which should reflect what Potuz mentioned)

author: terencechain
content:
Because the attesters will forward it to the proposer and the proposer would actually accept it. Vs. current gossip rule says you can only accept the first payload you have received.
But ya if this goes after second 0 of new slot then it is "too late"

author: luca_zanolini
content:
Yes exactly that is my understanding of the attack. But probably this addition is worth adding nevertheless? 

Do you know how is ExecutionPayloadEnvelopesByRoot used? I thought that this could be used to retrieve more  payloads in case the one you received was invalid

author: terencechain
content:
its for short range recovery.. examples...
- attestation pointing there's a full block, but i have not seen its payload..
- block building on top of full, but i cant sync the block because i dont have the payload
i dont think we have coded behavior that we assume a peer is malicious in a way and try to recover from an invalid payload. It's an interesting thought tho, and doable
